page: Vultr vx1-g-64c-256g is a general-purpose server instance within the VX1 Cloud Compute family. It features 64 vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC-Turin processor with an x86_64 architecture, operating at a clock speed of 2.0 GHz. The instance is configured with 256.0 GB of system memory, yielding a balanced 4.0 GB of RAM per core. The CPU allocation is shared, and the instance includes 1 GB of network-based local storage alongside one complimentary public IPv4 address.

In benchmark evaluations, the server demonstrates strong multi-core capabilities and memory throughput. The AMD EPYC-Turin processor achieves a top-tier rating in single-core stress-ng performance and a strong rating in multi-core stress-ng testing. Memory bandwidth tests show top-tier performance for large block read and write operations, reaching 530.35 GB/sec and 259.87 GB/sec respectively. Additionally, the instance performs in the top tier for single-threaded gzip compression and multi-threaded bzip3 decompression.

The hardware profile presents specific tradeoffs. While the 64 vCPUs and 256.0 GB RAM configuration offers high resource density, the CPU allocation is shared rather than dedicated, which may introduce performance variability under sustained workloads. Furthermore, local storage is limited to 1 GB of network storage, requiring external storage solutions for data-heavy applications. However, the shared CPU model provides a cost-efficient alternative for highly parallelized workloads that do not require constant, dedicated CPU execution.

This instance is suitable for workloads such as static web serving, database operations, and moderate-sized language model inference. It achieves strong benchmark results in static web serving throughput at 65.97 GB/sec and database operations. It also supports prompt processing and text generation for small and medium LLM models, making it viable for distributed applications, batch processing, and memory-intensive general-purpose workloads.
description: Vultr vx1-g-64c-256g is a general-purpose server instance in the VX1 Cloud Compute family, featuring 64 vCPUs and 256.0 GB of RAM. Powered by an AMD EPYC-Turin x86_64 processor running at 2.0 GHz, this instance utilizes a shared CPU allocation model. It includes 1 GB of network storage and one public IPv4 address. Benchmarks indicate top-tier single-core CPU performance, strong multi-core execution, and high memory bandwidth, with large block reads reaching 530.35 GB/sec. Tradeoffs include the shared CPU model and minimal local storage, which are balanced by qualitative cost efficiency for resource-dense deployments. The instance is suited for database operations, static web serving, and small-to-medium scale LLM inference.
